title: Container With Most Water
statement: Given a list of vertical line heights, choose two different positions as the container walls. The water held is the distance between the positions times the shorter of the two heights. Return the largest possible area.
constraints: 2 <= n <= 100000; 0 <= height[i] <= 10000. An O(n^2) pair check is correct but too slow at the upper bound; an O(n) solution is intended.

brute_force.approach: Try every pair of lines i and j, compute the width j - i, compute the limiting height min(height[i], height[j]), and keep the largest area found.

optimal.approach: Start with the widest possible pair, one line at each end. Compute its area, then move inward from the side with the smaller height. The shorter side is the current limit on water height; keeping it while reducing width cannot produce a better area, so all pairs that keep that shorter line and move only the taller side can be safely skipped. Repeat until the two sides meet.

key_insight: For any current pair, the shorter wall limits the area; if width is going to shrink, keeping that same shorter wall cannot help, so the only side worth moving is the shorter one.
ladder: identify which wall limits the current area
prove one side's candidates can be skipped

traps.why_wrong: The tallest pair may be close together, and width matters just as much as height.
traps.counterexample: height = [1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]; the two height-8 lines give area 40, but heights 8 and 7 at positions 1 and 8 give area 49.

traps.wrong_approach: Always moving the taller side inward.
traps.why_wrong: The shorter side still caps the height, and the width only gets smaller, so this can skip the best answer.
traps.counterexample: height = [1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]; starting at the ends and repeatedly moving the taller right side misses the area 49 pair.

traps.wrong_approach: Using the taller of the two lines as the water height.
traps.why_wrong: Water spills over the shorter wall, so the container height is the minimum of the two chosen heights.
traps.counterexample: height = [1,100]; using the taller height gives 100, but the real area is 1.

traps.wrong_approach: Stopping as soon as a very large area is found locally.
traps.why_wrong: A narrower pair can still win if its limiting height is much larger.
traps.counterexample: height = [1,2,100,100]; the end pair has area 3, but the best area is 100 from the two height-100 lines.

leak_terms: discard pairs using the current shorter wall
underlying_primitive: Safe elimination of many candidates by proving they cannot beat the current possibility when one limiting factor stays the same and another gets worse.
